serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of crab meat omelette fried rice is approximately 350 g, typically presented as a full plate at a Malaysian or Singaporean zi char restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice with Seafood — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table, adapted with USDA FoodData Central crab meat values
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition; USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for crab meat omelette fried rice as a composite dish. Values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD seafood fried rice entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central values for cooked crab meat (FDC ID 175180) and egg. Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of cooked jasmine rice (~55%), crab meat (~20%), egg (~15%), and seasonings/oil (~10%). Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Fried rice (white rice base)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crab meat omelette fried rice. GI of 54 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base, GI ~54, tested value from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). The addition of crab meat, egg, and oil lowers the effective GI relative to plain white rice (GI ~72) due to protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 75.25 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 40.6, rounded to 40.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from soy sauce and seasonings, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the recommended 2000 mg/day limit; request reduced-soy-sauce preparation if consuming.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice base (~40.7 GL per serving) with partial mitigation from protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions as the white rice base contributes a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving; the protein from crab meat and egg partially attenuates the glycemic response, but portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol content from egg and crab meat (~95 mg cholesterol per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its elevated sodium content and moderate dietary cholesterol from egg and crab; the fat profile is predominantly unsaturated which is relatively favourable, but sodium restriction remains the primary concern for cardiovascular risk management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab Meat Omelette Fried Rice is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean zi char (煮炒) dish consisting of wok-fried jasmine rice combined with fresh or frozen crab meat and a lightly beaten egg omelette, seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, white pepper, garlic, and spring onion. The dish is prepared in a very hot wok to achieve the characteristic 'wok hei' (breath of the wok) smoky flavour. The texture is fluffy and slightly crispy at the edges, with tender crab meat distributed throughout and soft egg ribbons folded in. It is a hearty, protein-rich main course typically served as part of a shared zi char meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 168 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and 21.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is notable for its high sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) from soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low GI), though the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is substantial (~40.7), requiring portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Crab meat contributes vitamin B12, selenium, and zinc. The dish contains crustacean, egg, and soy allergens. It is commonly found at Malaysian seafood restaurants and Singaporean zi char stalls, and is considered a premium fried rice variant due to the use of crab meat.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for crab meat omelette fried rice as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD seafood fried rice entries, FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice data, and USDA FoodData Central crab meat and egg values using a weighted ingredient composition model. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved GI source (fried rice, white rice base, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted sources for this dish type; crab meat contains trace vitamin D but the composite value is not reliably quantifiable from available approved sources. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the standard preparation. 3. Nutrient values represent a modelled composite (cooked jasmine rice ~55%, crab meat ~20%, egg ~15%, oil and seasonings ~10%) and carry an estimated uncertainty of ±15% on macronutrients. 4. The food_name spelling 'Omelette' corrects the input record's 'Omellete' (double-l typo). 5. Halal status is marked true as the standard preparation uses crab (permissible crustacean under most Malaysian halal standards) and no pork or alcohol-based ingredients; however, restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified. 6. GI category is 'low' (GI = 54, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from adapted composite sources and carry moderate uncertainty.
